last_excerpt: The word "Saturday" has a planet in it. Saturn's day. Most people never notice that. Monday has the moon, Sunday has the sun, and then the rest of them — Tuesday through Friday — got Norse gods, which feels like the week slowly losing prestige as it goes on, until Saturday circles back to a Roman planet and Sunday starts the whole thing over. I'm not going to look into that further. There's a sound outside that I think is a screen door but could also be a gate latch. They have the same pitch — that thin metallic slap. The difference is in the resonance after. A screen door buzzes. A gate latch just stops. I've been thinking about how scissors work. Not the cutting part — the pivot. The rivet or screw that holds the two halves together. Every pair of scissors is two knives that need each other. Alone, a single scissor blade can't cut paper cleanly. It just pushes the paper away. You need the opposition. The shearing force only exists in the relationship between the blades. That's — no, I was going to make that into something about arguments or marriages or something. I don't need to. Scissors are interesting enough as scissors. The rivet loosens over time. Cheap scissors go wobbly. The blades start folding paper instead of cutting it. You can tighten the screw but then they're stiff and hard to open. There's a narrow band where scissors are good scissors. A Saturday afternoon is a specific quality of time. Not morning ambition, not Sunday's anticipatory dread. Just a kind of plateau. You've already done whatever you were going to do or you've decided you're not going to. Someone is dragging something across pavement. A trash can, maybe. That hollow plastic scrape. I wonder what the first scissors looked like. I know spring scissors — the U-shaped kind, like sheep shears — are older than pivot scissors. So for a long time, scissors were just one piece of metal bent back on itself. The force came from the spring of the metal wanting to return to its shape. You squeezed them closed and they opened themselves. That's a different relationship entirely. Not two things held together by a rivet but one thing folded against itself. The shadow from the window frame has moved about a hand's width since I started thinking. That's maybe twenty minutes of planetary rotation made visible on a wall. It doesn't look like it's moving. You just check back and it's somewhere else.
